did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models including DCGAN for data augmentation, U-Net for lesion segmentation, and Custom CNN and Xception for skin cancer classification on the ISIC 2020 dataset. It also employs Grad-CAM for explainable AI visualization.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a deep learning pipeline using DCGAN, U-Net, Custom CNN, and Xception models on the ISIC 2020 dataset. It reports specific results, including a classification accuracy of 98.43%, and includes sections for Proposed Methodology, Results, and Evaluation Metrics, which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Detection Using Deep Learning Models and Grad-CAM XAI
journal: 2025 9th International Conference on Computing, Communication, Control and Automation (ICCUBEA)
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning pipeline for skin cancer detection, specifically distinguishing between melanoma and non-melanoma lesions, using the ISIC 2020 dataset. The methodology employs DCGAN for synthetic data augmentation to enhance model robustness and U-Net for precise lesion segmentation. Classification performance was evaluated by comparing a Custom CNN and the Xception model on a subset of 5000 images. Both classification models achieved a high accuracy of 98.43%. Additionally, Grad-CAM was integrated to provide visual interpretability and improve the transparency of the model's predictions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary malignant class studied; the dataset designates images with Melanoma as 'Positive' for binary classification.
The comparator class for binary classification; designated as 'Negative' in the ISIC 2020 dataset used, encompassing benign lesions or other skin conditions.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between Melanoma (Positive) and Non-Melanoma (Negative/Benign) lesions using Custom CNN and Xception models on the ISIC 2020 dataset. The primary task is classification, achieving 98.43% accuracy. While U-Net is used for lesion segmentation (a secondary task for localization/preprocessing), the main predictive output evaluated is the disease label.

U-Net is employed to segment the pre-processed dataset and localize the Rate of Interest (lesion) for the classification models. This is a preprocessing step rather than the primary diagnostic prediction task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020: Skin Lesion Analysis towards Melanoma detection
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 33,126 dermoscopic images of skin lesions in .JPG format, linked to patient details such as age, sex, and anatomical location. The study utilized a subset of 5,000 images from this dataset for the proposed deep learning pipeline.
classes_used: Non-Melanoma (Negative/Benign lesions or other skin conditions)

use.training: A subset of 5,000 images was used for training the Custom CNN and Xception models, combined with real, pre-processed, and DCGAN-augmented images.
use.other: The full dataset (33,126 images) served as the source from which the 5,000-image subset was drawn for the study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests for the comparison between the Custom CNN and Xception models. It reports identical accuracy (98.43%) for both models but does not provide statistical validation of this equivalence or difference.

Custom CNN.architecture_details: Lightweight CNN with two convolution layers, two max_pooling layers, two dense layers, and a 20% dropout layer. Uses ReLU and Softmax activation functions.

Xception.architecture_details: Transfer learning model with fourteen block layers, including two Dense layers, one GlobalAveragePooling2D layer, and layers using Convolution, BatchNormalization, and Activation functions. Some layers were frozen during training.

ISIC 2020.notes: Both Custom CNN and Xception models achieved an accuracy of 98.43% on the subset of 5000 images from the ISIC 2020 dataset after 10 epochs. The study also integrated Grad-CAM for visual interpretability, highlighting that the 'Purple region' in heatmaps represented areas with the greatest impact on determining whether a lesion was benign or malignant.

architecture_used.pretraining: Xception model was pre-trained (transfer learning); Custom CNN was trained from scratch.

components: Layers using Convolution, BatchNormalization, and Activation functions
Xception.training_modification: Some layers were frozen during training

U-Net.structure: U-shaped architecture with Contracting Path (Encoder) and Expanding Path (Decoder)

classification_or_output_head.Xception: GlobalAveragePooling2D followed by Dense layers for binary classification

preprocessing_techniques: Image normalization: Transforming images into the range of (0-1) by taking into account the image size.
Data augmentation: Applying transformations including rotation, flipping (horizontal and vertical), scaling (zooming), and cropping to increase dataset size and diversity.
Image segmentation: Using U-Net architecture to segment the pre-processed dataset and localize the Rate of Interest (lesion) for classification models.
Data augmentation via GAN: Using DCGAN to create synthetic images that are coupled with the augmented real dataset to improve model generalization.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied transformations including cropping, zooming, rotation, and horizontal and vertical flipping to increase dataset size and diversity.

Used Deep Convolutional Generative Adversarial Networks (DCGAN) to create high-quality synthetic dermoscopic images. The DCGAN consists of a Generator Network (creating synthetic images from random noise) and a Discriminator Network (distinguishing real from synthetic). The synthetic images were coupled with the augmented real dataset to improve model generalization and robustness.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning pipeline, utilizing DCGAN for data augmentation, U-Net for lesion segmentation, and Custom CNN/Xception for classification, achieved a classification accuracy of 98.43% on a subset of 5,000 images from the ISIC 2020 dataset.
Both the Custom CNN and Xception models demonstrated comparable high performance (98.43% accuracy) in distinguishing between melanoma and non-melanoma lesions after 10 epochs of training.
The integration of DCGAN for synthetic data generation improved model robustness and dataset diversity, helping to address data scarcity and overfitting issues common in medical imaging.
The use of U-Net for precise lesion segmentation (localization of the Rate of Interest) contributed to improved classification performance by providing focused input to the classification models.

The integration of Grad-CAM (Explainable AI) successfully enhanced model transparency and interpretability by generating visual heatmaps that highlight the specific regions of the image contributing to the classification decision, thereby increasing trustworthiness for clinical verification.
important_remarks: The study uses only a small subset (5,000 images) of the ISIC 2020 dataset (33,126 total images), which limits the generalizability and robustness of the findings compared to using the full dataset.

Both the Custom CNN and Xception models achieved identical accuracy (98.43%) without reporting other critical metrics such as precision, recall, F1-score, or AUC-ROC, making it impossible to assess class balance or diagnostic trade-offs.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the equivalence or difference between the Custom CNN and Xception models, despite claiming a comparison analysis.
The paper does not specify the train/validation/test split ratios or the random seed used, raising concerns about data leakage or non-reproducible results.
The dataset includes patient demographics (age, sex, anatomical location), but the study does not report any analysis of demographic bias or performance stratification across these subgroups.

The use of DCGAN for data augmentation introduces potential risks of mode collapse or generating synthetic samples that do not accurately reflect real-world pathological variations, which could artificially inflate performance metrics.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope and lacks external validation on independent datasets, limiting its clinical applicability and reliability.
The input image size is reduced to 128x128 pixels, which may result in loss of fine-grained dermoscopic details crucial for accurate melanoma detection.
